exceed +50. Passive Dodge may not exceed +50.
ply to the use of ranged weapons, such as the use of a sling in a low cave. The Battle Expertise: Restricted Quarters Fighting skill can be used to offset these penalties.

Example: Jakko, a human warrior, chases

some goblins into a cave. Jakko is 6' tall with a 4' sword, so his melee range is (6/2) + 4 = 7'.

At first the 10' cave is fine, but as he continues to chase the goblins, it narrows to 6' high and wide. Now his head is brushing the ceiling, he cannot raise his sword over his head, and he has to be wary of clipping walls. The GM de cides this is “close” and imposes a -25 penalty.

Chasing the goblins further into a 4' high and wide section, Jakko is now crouched over, and his swing is further impeded to “cramped” and -50. Further on he enters into a 3' high and wide section, and is now using one hand to half crawl and reduced to waving his blade in nar row arcs for “tight” or -75. Finally he crawls into a 2' high and wide section, he moves on his belly, and he is reduced to cramped stabbing motions, so the GM rules the conditions “con fined” or -100. Note that the goblins would likely have no problems in the 6' section (unless wielding long pole arms), but even they would be struggling with restricted quarters by the time the cave is reduced to 2'.

Surprised or Flatfooted

When executing attacks on surprised foes, the attacker gets +25 OB, in addition to any posi tional modifiers (if melee). Against flatfooted foes, the bonus is +60: +25 for surprise and +35 for an automatic rear attack, regardless of position. (See Section 8.3 for the definition of surprise and flatfooted.) This represents the combatant being able to freely hit his foe

Restricted Quarters wherever desired, even if in front. In addition,

a flatfooted foe cannot benefit from a shield, although As a general rule, if a combatant is fighting in an area any magical or armor quality bonuses would still ap smaller than his melee range, he should suffer a penalty ply. Note that ranged weapons do not get the automat due to restricted quarters. This is due to a smaller area ic rear attack bonus since ranged weapons do not get to maneuver in, difficulty in swinging a weapon, etc. positional modifiers. Surprised foes still get DB due to Clearance is not as important as one’s ability to maneu Quickness, but flatfooted combatants do not. ver, but for some long weapons it can be a more serious

Example: Ord, an assassin of vile disposition, has

problem. ROLEMASTER become angered during a card game with the ev

It is ultimately up to the Gamemaster to determine the UNIFIED er-cheerful Mhairi. Determined to have his revenge,  penalty based on how restricted the combat is, taking Ord bides his time and ambushes Mhairi in the dead Arms Law into account the size of the room or area, allies, foes, of night as she walks home. Ord catches the slightly ceiling height, furniture, weapon type being wielded, drunk Mhairi completely flatfooted, giving him a etc. There are four categories of Restricted Quarters: bonus of +60. Fortunately for the ever-lucky Mhai Close (-25), Cramped (-50), Tight (-75), and Confined ri, poor Ord rolls and fumbles his sword, dropping (-100). The Restricted Quarters penalties could also ap it on his foot and alerting Mhairi. Foiled again! 167

9.6. Defensive Bonus

A combatant’s Defensive Bonus (DB) represents dodging, parrying, shield use, and tactical advantag es. A combatant’s DB is determined by the factors in Table 9-5. As long as a combatant is able to move it always get its DB from Quickness, unless caught flat footed. A flying combatant gets a bonus to its DB even in melee, as the extra dimension of movement is use ful in dodging attacks. To use a shield, the combatant must be aware of the attack and be using the shield to defend against it.

It is possible to have negative DB due to a Quickness penalty. This does not represent foes jumping into the path of an arrow. Rather, the attack tables assume an average foe who is actively trying to not get hit. If the combatant is deficient in some way, attacks will be more effective against it than against an average foe.

Cover

ers against both melee and ranged attacks, and these are given in Table 9-5. Note that cover provides twice the modification against ranged attacks as it does against melee.

Hard cover consists of anything that can stop the attack outright (e.g., walls and trees) or deflect the shot (e.g., fences and bushes). Soft cover consists of anything that obscures vision, but does not interfere with the attack (e.g., fog, darkness, curtains). The GM is the final arbi ter on how much cover an obstacle offers and whether the cover is hard or soft cover, based on the nature of the attack and the obstacle being used for cover.

To determine the amount of cover, consider how much of the defender is blocked from the attacker’s view by the cover. If the attacker cannot see a quarter of the target, that is partial cover, if he cannot see half of the target, that is half cover, and if the target is 90% or more obscured, that qualifies as full cover. Small characters should be able to more easily find sufficient cover to trouble finding anything big enough to hide behind.

ROLEMASTER on positioning and the relative size of the combatants.

UNIFIED

For example, attacks from the second rank are normally  Arms Law considered half hard cover, for a +40 DB bonus. Pole arms may be used more effectively when striking past cover and reduce the cover bonuses by half (i.e., half hard cover results in only +20 DB).

Dodging

A combatant may attempt to dodge out of the way of attacks, increasing their Defensive Bonus.

There are three types of dodging, representing differ ent amounts of effort spent on defense. A combatant may benefit from only one type of dodging in a round.

All types of dodging add to DB against all melee foes the combatant is aware of for the entire round.

Dodging can also be used against ranged attacks, but the DB value is halved, as the combatant must move away from where they anticipate the attack will go, or simply move in an unpredictable manner in order to be difficult to target; these attacks are typically too fast to focus on dodging the projectile itself. Dodging is not possible at all against area attacks.

Dodging utilizes the ranks and bonus of one of the Movement skills, depending on the environment. In normal land-based encounters use Running, in the wa ter use Swimming, and while climbing use Climbing.

If the combatant is currently moving using Acrobatics, Walls, curtains, fog and other combatants can all make the GM may allow that to be used as the appropriate it difficult to target a foe. Cover can provide DB modifi Movement skill.

Passive dodging takes no action and adds the combat ant’s ranks in the appropriate Movement skill to their DB, up to a maximum of +50 DB. Passive dodging may not be combined with DB granted from passive use of a shield. Using the shield involves its own type of footwork as is represented by the Shield skill.

Partial dodging requires concentration and adds half the combatant’s appropriate Movement skill to their DB.

Full dodging takes 4 Action Points and adds the com batant’s full appropriate Movement skill to their DB.

Encumbrance and armor penalties greatly hinder dodging as they apply to the combatant's gained DB.

These penalties apply in full to passive, partial, and full dodging, which means any moderate encumbrance or armor is often enough to nullify the much smaller passive bonus. If dodging ranged attacks, subtract all penalties first before halving the DB. protect themselves, while Big characters may have The DB bonuses for partial and full dodging are re duced by any injury penalties which the character is Another combatant (enemy or ally) counts as hard cov suffering. As with other actions, partial and full dodg er if someone attempts to attack past one combatant to ing are also reduced by the pace penalty if the charac strike another combatant. The amount of cover is based ter is moving.

Parrying

Combat in Rolemaster is quite deadly, and using parry is one of the best ways for a combatant to stay alive.

Combatants who try to go full offense and never parry will be quite dangerous for the short time before they take a lethal critical and die. Generally, only mind less or berserk combatants will completely disregard danger in order to strike out at a foe with all of their offense.

A combatant may trade offensive capability for de possible.

If the combatant who is parrying is making multiple attacks that round, his OB for all his attacks is reduced by the same amount, although it is only added to the DB once.

As long as the last Action Point a combatant spent on actions was on a melee action, he may parry any foe who attacks him in melee. (Spending an Action Point on movement does not interrupt melee or pre vent parrying.) If the attacker is to his rear or flank the OB is penalized by the positional modifier (Table 9-5) before any amount of OB is diverted to DB, although that penalty may be offset by the Combat Expertise: Reverse Strike skill.

Example: Niles is training several young monks and one of them is behind him. Niles’ unarmed OB is +90, of which he will parry with 50. Normally, this would be completely eliminated due to the -70 penalty for attacks (and parry) to the rear, but Niles has +50 Reverse Strike skill. The penalty is reduced to -20, so 30 of his parry will be applied against the young monk’s attack.

A combatant may attempt to parry more than one foe, reducing the OB by the desired amount against each foe. An Absolute maneuver roll is required for each foe to be parried after the first (using the combatant’s Combat Training skill), and with a Success or higher the foe may be parried (otherwise no effect). A maneu ver roll is allowed for each additional foe even if an earlier such roll was failed. If a combatant uses all of his OB for parrying, so that his OB is zero by the time his attack would resolve, no attack is made.

Example: Anika is fighting a wolf with two attacks (bite and claw). Anika’s OB is +100 with her falchion. She chooses to parry the wolf with 30.

As a result, her OB is reduced to +70, but her DB increases by 30 against both attacks. The goblin rid ing the wolf is also attacking with a spear. If Anika succeeds in an Absolute maneuver with her Blades skill, she could decide to parry the goblin with +20, but that would reduce her OB to +50. Decisions, decisions. fense by spending some of his available Offensive Bo Restricted Parrying nus to parry a foe when attacked. His DB is increased In some situations, it can be very difficult to parry, by an equal amount and counts against all melee at such as an unarmed combatant parrying an opponent tacks from that foe for the round. If he has no OB left, with a weapon. If parrying is restricted, only 1 point of because it was already spent on an attack or parrying DB is added for every 2 points of OB spent (round up). others, then he may not parry. No penalties apply to All other parry rules apply as normal. If multiple sit the parry DB directly, but injury penalties, pace, and uations apply to make parrying exceptionally difficult other penalties will reduce the available Offensive Bo the GM may rule that parrying cannot be applied at all. nus and therefore limit the amount of parrying that is Restricted parrying includes the following situations: • An unarmed combatant parrying against someone with a weapon, unless the unarmed combatant has an item or armor with which to parry (e.g., brac ers, small shield, or a large metal mug).

• A combatant fighting an invisible foe, because he cannot see blows coming, so he can only guess at where to parry to cover vital areas.

• A combatant fighting an incorporeal foe because blocking is ineffective and he can only rely on motion to avoid getting hit.

• GM discretion: The Gamemaster may rule additional situations that restrict one’s ability to parry (e.g., a small halfling with a dagger parrying a gigantic dragon claw attack).

Example: Xavier, having just broken his heirloom sword, is left with nothing but his own hands and his skill at Martial Arts Strikes when he is set upon by a fearsome war mattock-wielding ogre! Because he has nothing with which to parry, Xavier is re stricted in his parrying. He decides to parry 80, which gives him +40 DB instead of the normal +80.

If he had been more thoughtful, he might have held onto the hilt of his broken sword; it would have been enough to use for more effective parrying.

ROLEMASTER

UNIFIED



Arms Law

Shields

Like armor, the use of shields is varied, from small target shields to large wall shields weighing 20 lbs.

Larger shields can protect the most but are bulky and heavy. Very skilled combatants can use even smaller shields to great effect, especially when confronted with a single foe.

A shield may be used against one attack which the combatant is aware of and facing in his front arc.

Larger shields may be used against more than one at tack, up to a maximum number of attacks based on the shield type as shown in Table 9-6. For each attack be yond the first, a maneuver roll must be made using the combatant’s Shield skill. On a Success or higher the DB may be applied, otherwise it is not. A failed roll still counts against the maximum number of attacks.

Shield DB may be applied against melee, missile, and directed spell attacks.

Additionally, a combatant may add his Shield skill ranks to his DB, for a maximum of +50 DB due to shield (including the base shield DB). This bonus ap plies against the same attacks as the base shield DB.

This passive blocking may not be combined with pas sive dodging, as using the shield involves its own type of footwork as is represented by the Shield skill.

By spending actions, a shield may be used even more effectively to block melee or ranged attacks. Partial blocking requires concentration and adds half the combatant’s Shield skill to their DB. Full blocking takes 4 Action Points and adds the combatant’s full ROLEMASTER Shield skill to their DB. The bonuses for partial and UNIFIED full blocking are reduced by any injury penalties the  character has sustained, and by any pace penalty if the Arms Law character is moving. As above, a Shield maneuver roll is required for each attack after the first. Only one type of blocking (partial, full, or passive) may be used per round.

A shield used to attack (a shield bash) does not pro vide any DB whatsoever for the round.

Example: Anika has a full shield, 15 ranks of Shield skill, and a bonus of +78. She uses her shield against the spear of a goblin and the bite of the wolf he is riding upon, as well as an arrow fired by a goblin archer. She intends to attack, so she chooses to only benefit from passive blocking. Anika’s DB increases by 25 + 15 = 40. She gains this benefit for the first attack automatically, but must succeed in a Shield absolute maneuver for each of the other attacks to use the shield against them.

Example: Later, Anika is facing three goblin ar chers. She chooses a full block as she is advancing on their position, costing her 4 AP but hopefully keeping her alive long enough to attack them next round. Her bonus is 25 + 78 = +103, but, as before, she must succeed in a Shield absolute maneuver to defend against the second and third arrows. Never theless, she is grateful she left her little target shield at home and brought the full shield!

Weapons and Items as “Shields” Weapons, or other suitably robust items, can be used for passive defense. A combatant can use a sword to attack and parry, and a stool in the off-hand as addi tional defense. If not used to attack or parry, items give a +5 DB vs. one melee attack. Such items cannot be used with Shield skill for passive, partial, or full blocking. Waving a dagger around will not protect you from incoming arrows! It is best to find cover, move evasively, or find a shield.

Table 9-6: Shields
Type DB Max Attacks
Target +15 1
Normal +20 2
Full +25 3
Wall +30 4

9.7. Special Maneuvers

Called Shots

Sometimes a combatant may wish to attack a spe cific location on the body, such as the head. The five locations on the critical tables are head (in cluding neck), arms, chest, abdomen (including groin), and legs, and limbs each have a right and a left. Making a called shot incurs a penalty due to the difficulty of hitting the specific location.

Against same-sized humanoids, a called shot usually incurs a penalty of -25 to the attack roll. The GM may decide in some circumstances to impose a greater or lesser penalty (from -50 to -10). For example, a human melee attacking a giant’s right leg with a dagger may only be a -10, while attacking his head may be -50.

Called shots are most useful when the targeted area is less armored than the remainder of the body. If the tar geted area is armored differently from the torso, treat the attack as being against AT 1 for no armor, AT 3 for light armor, AT 5 for medium armor, and AT 9 for heavy armor.

If the attack is successful, then the attack has hit the specified location. If a critical is inflicted, make the critical roll as normal, but if the rolled result indicates a different location, keep checking lesser results on that column until you reach one that affects the called body area, and resolve using that result. If there is no such lesser row, then the strike was on target but mostly inef fective; use the result on the column for an 01 result and modify the text to fit. The Groin & Abdomen result on a 66 cannot be obtained by this shift (it may still be used if rolled naturally); use the 26-35 result instead.

Disarming

tempts are resolved as a conflicting maneuver, with the disarmer rolling as if it were a normal attack (including any penalty from the target parrying the attacker), with additional penalties as indicated on Table 9-7a. The defending foe makes a defense roll (which does not re quire an action), as if it were an attack roll (subtracting any parry used), with any amount over 100 used as an additional penalty to the attacker’s disarm roll or any amount under 100 applied as a bonus. The result is in dicated on Table 9-7a.

Learning techniques to disarm can help in the disarm ing attempt. Combatants can use the Combat Expertise: Disarming skill bonus to reduce the disarming penalties (except for the foe’s defense roll), to a minimum pen alty of 0.

Table 9-7a: Disarm Maneuvers
Result
< 1 Absolute Failure
Your attempt to disarm your foe is laughable. Not only do you
fail to come close to disarming him, but you immediately fum
ble your weapon.
1 - 75 Failure
Sigh. Some days it just seems like the world is out to get you.
You miss your opportunity. You fail to disarm. You fail to at
tack. You just fail. You're a failure.
UM 66 Unusual Event
Weird… it seems your foe is trying to disarm you, perhaps in
advertently, at the same time. If you fail this maneuver, your foe
has disarmed you. If you succeed, you still manage to disarm
your foe, but drop your weapon as well.
76 - 100 Partial Success
You can't partially disarm someone! Instead treat the disarm as
a normal attack using this roll, but using all normal modifiers
(and parry).
101 - 175 Success
You successfully disarm your foe! The weapon falls to the
ground (and must make a breakage check), but is within melee
range.
176 + Absolute Success
You make disarming look easy as your foe gawks at you as
you expertly remove him from his weapon. You may choose
between taking the weapon for yourself or throw it up to 10' in
a direction of your choice.
Modifiers
-50 Unarmed/grapple/crush attack
-75 Blade attack
-100 Piercing attack
-100 Ranged attack*
-25 Disarming a 2-H weapon*
* cumulative with other penalties

The penalties for disarming a 2-H weapon, the foe’s A disarming attack seeks to force an opponent to drop defense roll, and using a ranged weapon are cumulative his weapon, or even take it from him. Disarming at with all other penalties.

Disarming cannot be used against natural attacks; at best one can make a called shot on that body part and hope to incapacitate or sever the attacking limb.

Example: The angry peasant rushes at Meretor with

a wooden club and misinformed accusations. Em barrassed but with no desire to harm the peasant, Meretor attempts to disarm him instead. Meretor is armed with a broadsword (-75 for disarming). His ROLEMASTER melee skill is +87 and his Disarm skill is +40 (re UNIFIED ducing the penalty to -35). He puts faith in his abil  ities and elects not to parry, so his total is +52. He Arms Law rolls 41. The peasant has +20 melee skill and rolls a 30. Since the peasant’s roll has fallen under 100, the amount below 100 (50) is added to Meretor’s roll for a total of 143. The peasant’s club tumbles to the ground.

Firing into Melee

There is nothing preventing a combatant from firing or throwing a weapon into melee, whether to hit a foe amongst allies or pick out a specific foe in a battle.

Based on the position of the attacker, defender, and other combatants the defender may gain cover from the other combatants.

Example: Two characters are in melee. An archer is trying to shoot past his ally to hit an enemy. The GM decides that the target has partial hard cover based on the positions of the three combatants, and this gives the target +40 DB for cover. ants. Fumbles are ignored on the second attack roll.

Katas

Most often, a combatant is wielding a single weap on and attacking a single opponent. However, on occasion, a combatant may be using multiple attacks together (multiple weapons or natural attacks). These simultaneous attacks are often learned by practicing combinations of moves that allow the motions of the attacks to flow together, each alongside or into the next, referred to as katas. These moves are performed either simultaneously or in rapid succession, allowing multiple attacks to be resolved.

To execute multiple attacks, the use of each attack must be justified with respect to a given limb, with no limb used as the basis for more than one attack. For example, a combatant wielding two weapons, with one in each hand, may strike once with each of them.

A combatant could also make a martial arts sweep with a leg or a strike with the head (i.e., a head butt or horns), but cannot use either to make a dagger attack unless they have somehow mounted blades on their boot and helmet, which would be awkward enough to add a significant penalty to OB.

A weapon wielded in a combatant’s off-hand suffers a -20 penalty (unless they have the Ambidextrous talent)

ROLEMASTER and uses the same skill as that weapon in their primary UNIFIED hand. Natural, unarmed attacks, and shield attacks do  Arms Law not suffer any penalty for use of the off-hand.

When making two simultaneous attacks, there is a penalty of -75 to each attack. For each attack beyond two, this penalty increases by -25, as shown in Table 9-7b. Furthermore, there is a penalty of -20 for each opponent beyond the first, also applied to all attacks.

The penalties (including the multiple opponent penal ty) can be offset by the combatant’s Combat Expertise: Multiple Attacks skill.

The penalties for katas are also offset by modifiers for light weapons, but note that the total modifier can never result in a net bonus to the attack, only in can celling out the penalties. A light weapon is a dagger, unarmed attack, natural weapon (e.g., claws, bite), or any weapon with a -1 size adjustment (e.g., epee, machete, hand axe). If the combatant is making more than two attacks, the bonus for a light off-hand weap on only applies once, and only if all attacks beyond the If a ranged attack misses its intended target then the first are qualified. For example, a combatant attacking attack may randomly hit one of the combatants that with a sword, dagger, and a kick would receive the provided cover. In this case, make a second attack roll +20 bonus, but a combatant striking with two swords at +0 skill OB against the secondary target. The new and a kick would not. target (select randomly if more than one) does not gain Note that while katas allow multiple attacks as part the benefit of any cover modifiers from other combat of a single attack action, a combatant may also make multiple attacks simply by performing multiple attack actions, either by using fewer Action Points for each attack or if they have extra Action Points (e.g., due to a Haste effect). The penalties on table 9-7b apply only to attacks made as part of the same attack action.

Example: Kulahren wants to attack one foe with his dagger and another with his rapier. Two attacks are -75 each, and attacking two foes gives an addition al -20. This -95 is reduced by Kulahren’s Multiple Attack skill of +59 (including a +20 bonus because his off-hand weapon is a dagger) resulting in a -95 + 59 = -36 to both attacks. If he wanted to attack only one foe with both weapons they would each have a penalty of -75 + 59 = -16 instead.

Table 9-7b: Katas
# Attacks Modifier
2 -75
3 -100
4 -125
5 -150
Per additional foe -20 each
Primary weapon is light +10
Off-hand weapon is:
Dagger or any natural weapon +20
Any other light weapon +10

Mounted Combat

Being mounted provides an enormous tactical advantage.

Mounted combatants benefit from the animal’s speed and mass when charging, as well as a higher ground bonus when they are fighting similarly-sized opponents on foot. Charging attacks made by mounted opponents are treated as the size of the mount plus any increases due to charging speed (based on the BMR of the mount). The mount and rider are considered separate combatants, with separate initiatives and actions. Fumbles made in mounted combat should be rolled on the ‘Mounted Com bat’ column.

However, riders attacking from a moving mount are sub ject to a Pace Penalty based on the pace of the mount (-100 for Sprint, -125 for Dash). These penalties can be reduced with the Battle Expertise: Mounted Combat skill.

Controlling a mount is assumed to be a negligible activ ity under normal circumstances; however, the GM may require a Riding maneuver for unusual maneuvers or situations. Riders with zero riding skill ranks are the ex ception to this rule, and attempts to make attacks from a moving mount should require a medium riding maneuver at the normal -25 no skill penalty plus mount pace each round an attack is declared from a moving mount, with failure resulting in the rider falling off.

Note that mounts not trained for combat will very likely panic and flee when fighting breaks out. Rules for mount morale and fear are covered in Section 14.3.

Protecting Others

Sometimes a combatant may want to parry or use a shield to protect an ally. He can do so by sacrificing some offensive capability to impose himself between the other combatants. The protector must be within melee range of both of the combatants, and gets a penalty to his OB based on his position as indicated on Table 9-7c.

The penalties can be offset by the combatant’s Battle Expertise: Protect skill. The protector may designate any part of his parry or shield DB as protecting the defended combatant instead of himself. The protector may wait until the attack is declared before deciding to do so.

Example: Anika and Talen are side by side facing a

massive ogre with a +150 OB. They both allocate 80 to parry, which is probably not enough to stop the ogre’s blow. However, as the ogre begins to swing his huge axe at Talen, Anika decides she will protect her ally. She has +25 in the Protect skill, offsetting half the penalty of -50. Thus, Anika takes a -25 to her OB (lowering her parry to +55) and parries the remainder on behalf of Talen. Talen now has a DB of 135 (80 from his own parry + 55 from Anika). Talen now has a decent chance to survive, but Anika has left herself open to any attacks direct ed toward her.

Table 9-7c: Protect
Scenario Modifier
Protector between Attacker and Defender -25
Defender between Attacker and Protector -100
All other scenarios -50



Arms Law

Slaying Attacks

Some attacks (often magi cal) are exceptionally dan gerous and carry a higher chance of severe injury.

Similarly, some creatures may be particularly vul nerable to a type of attack (e.g., fire attacks vs. an ice elemental). These are referred to as Slaying at tacks and are one of the

Slaying and also give the type. the critical roll.

penalty cannot be offset by the Subdual skill.

Subdual attacks do half concussion hit damage and use the Subdual critical table instead of the normal critical type, with a maximum severity of E.

Example: The ever-talented Karn has a Subdual skill bonus of +100. In melee combat, he could make a subdual attack with any type of weapon without penalty. However, Karn’s favorite mode of attack is from afar. He will often try to subdue using a short bow, with which he gets a -50 to all subdual attacks. few effects that give a direct bonus to the critical roll (-100 for piercing, -50 for ranged, total penalty of as indicated in Table 9-7d. For other known races and creatures, Quickness, magical armor, applicable spells, etc. still the Subdual skill is assumed to include subdual tech applies. A foe who has declared parry on the caster niques, or at least a rough knowledge of vulnerable gets full benefit of the parry to their DB for this type of attack.

9.8. Criticals & Injuries

An injury is all of the hits inflicted as well as criticals delivered as the result of an attack, failed maneuver, or spell. Hits represent light injuries (bruises, cuts), general pain, minor blood loss, and the will to keep fighting. Criticals represent types of damage causing more serious wounds that can include stun, broken and severed limbs, and major blood loss.

There are a variety of critical tables representing dif ferent types of damage (e.g., Slash, Puncture, Heat, etc.), each with 5 levels of severity from A to E. A criticals are the least severe and usually result in minor damage, with an incapacitating injury or death only occurring in very rare cases (i.e., a roll of 100). E criti cals tend to be moderate to severe, with a good chance of death or incapacitation, although minor blows can result. Each critical result will give a description and a series of numbers and symbols. (e.g., “Foe reels from the blow. +5 hits; stunned 1 round”). ended) roll, usually without modifiers. Slaying at tacks, Ambush attacks, exceptional attacks over 175, and possibly magical effects can modify the critical roll, but these tend to be rare.

Although the critical tables have a maximum severity of E, the attack tables may indicate results as severe as J. Criticals higher than an E do an E critical plus a secondary critical as shown in Table 9-8a. Depending on the type of attack the type of the secondary critical (e.g., Slash, Krush, etc.) may not be the same. If the at tack table lists multiple types of criticals, they are used in the order listed. If multiple criticals are delivered against a target for any reason, all criticals always use the same roll and modifiers, and consequently will hit the same target location (so a flaming sword will cut and burn the target’s arm, rather than cutting his arm and burning his foot).

The severity of a critical can also be increased or de creased due to size differences or other factors. If a critical is reduced below an A severity, find the next lower result that affects the same area of the body on the A column and apply it. If the critical is reduced more than 1 below an A, move down again to the next lower result for each step less than A. If there is no lower result for that area then use the lowest (01 roll) critical result and change the description as necessary.

Example: An A Krush critical is scored and the roll is 70, but the severity is reduced due to use of a small weapon against a medium target. A result of 67-75 is a Leg hit, so the next lower Leg result (for 36-45) is used instead: “Weak delivery, but great timing. Foe caught off balance.”

Example: Another A Krush critical is rolled in the same circumstance, with a result of 8, another Leg shot. There is no lesser Leg result, so the result of 1 is used instead. Since it indicates “Blow barely grazes foe’s ear,” the GM tells the player “the blow barely grazes your knee” instead.

When an area effect causes criticals to multiple tar gets, the result for each target should be rolled sepa rately, so it is possible

Table 9-8a: High Criticals
F E + A
G E + B
H E + C
I E + C + A
J E + C + B
for a Fire Ball to burn
one target’s arm and
another target’s foot.

The GM may choose to roll a single time for a large group, or for sections of a large group, to save time.

(I.e., if boiling water pours over a hundred soldiers inflicting a B Heat critical on each of them, the GM may choose to roll one critical for all 100, or perhaps Criticals are resolved with a straight d100 (non-open- five groups of 20 to keep some variation.)

ROLEMASTER

UNIFIED



Arms Law

Interpreting Criticals

Critical results assume a humanoid opponent, which can easily be adjusted for most beings. Across all crit ical tables and severities, for a given roll, the location is always the same (e.g., a roll of 56-65 is always an arm hit). In the case more guidance is needed, use the rough rules below. leg, head, chest, and abdomen (lower abdomen and groin). It is often necessary to know on which side of the body the hit has been scored (partic ularly with arm and leg hits). If the unmodified attack roll is odd, the hit has been on the foe’s left side, while if the unmodified attack roll is even, the hit has been on his right side.

• Death: Any critical that specifies death should be considered as causing death, regardless of the tar get. For example, if a skeleton who does not have a brain receives a critical result that says “brain is destroyed,” the skeleton’s head is destroyed and it is “dead” anyway. Any specific exceptions to this rule will be noted in the creature’s description in Creature Law. the left-right rule, then randomly determine which limb was hit. arm locations (determine left-right then random ize).

• No such location: Snakes, amoebas, and certain other creatures might not have a specific location that matches the given critical location. In these cases, change the text of the critical to affect the torso or other appropriate part, while keeping the actual results of the critical.

• Tail: Tails are sensitive areas, being an extension of the spine, so the tail is considered part of the abdomen. If it is plausible that the tail has been hit in combat (e.g., frontal attack in a narrow corridor is not), use odd/even to determine abdomen/tail.

ROLEMASTER

UNIFIED For a 66 result, randomly determine if it is the ab

 domen or tail.

Arms Law

• Wings: For birds and other creatures with no arms, wings will equate to arms. However, if the foe has arms and wings, use the left-right rule then ran domly determine if it is an arm or a wing.

Criticals can cause a variety of effects from fatigue to bleeding to long-term damage or death. The text of a critical will describe the hit, and sometimes a specific effect such as dropping an item. All types of standard critical results are described below, although some critical results may additionally describe a more unique effect.

• Location: Criticals occur in one of 5 locations: arm, Bleeding

Bleeding too serious to stop on its own will cause additional hits every round until death or until it is stopped through first aid or magic. Even a wound bleeding at 1 hit/round will cause death within min utes. Minor bleeding wounds that clot on their own are part of generic hit damage taken. Bleeding is sub tracted from hits at the end of every round (during the upkeep phase).

Breakage

Some criticals will trigger a breakage roll (Section 6.1) in the location hit, sometimes at a penalty. This could be armor, a shield, or an item carried at that lo cation. If the GM decides there are multiple options • Multiples of the same limb pair: If the foe has mul for breakage, randomly determine which one of the tiple sets of arms or legs, determine the side using possible items in that area requires a breakage roll.

If the critical indicates an item is dropped it must make an immediate breakage roll. In addition to determining • Non-humanoids: If the foe is non-humanoid then if the item has been damaged or broken, the ones die shift the location to the most analogous location of the breakage roll is how far away the item has land on the foe. A quadruped’s hind legs would be leg ed, in feet. The GM can overrule this if it does not locations while the front legs would be arm loca make sense (e.g., an anvil will always land near your tions. An octopus’ tentacles would be the leg and feet, but a ball may end up far away).

Fatigue

Fatigue can represent anything from getting the wind knocked out of you to getting out-maneuvered (there by requiring additional energy with which to attack or defend). Fatigue penalties are effective immediately and can be lowered by normal means (e.g., rest, Abso lute Success on an Endurance roll, spells, etc.).

Keep in mind that fatigue results in excess of -50 total should be converted into an injury, which takes effect immediately. The specifics of the fatigue injury should be determined by the GM, based on the critical or fumble that caused the penalty to increase past -50.

Grapple

Grapples are often due to unarmed attacks such as wrestling, but can also occur from nets, bolas, or other entangling attacks. Grapple results are given as a per centage representing how effectively the defender has been grabbed, held, or entangled. The grapple % is a penalty to all actions for the defender until he breaks free. Grappling is cumulative, so a 30% grapple re sult followed by a 10% grapple results in the defender grappled for 40% and making actions at -40.

The defender can attempt to break or slip free of a grapple using the rules for contested Feats of Strength (Section 5.2) using his Wrestling, Contortions, or other appropriate grappling skill, with the defender subject to the full grapple penalty. If the grappler is an object such as a net rather than a combatant, treat the escape attempt as a Medium maneuver, otherwise use the opponent’s weight to determine the difficulty. Any attempt to break free from a grapple is a 2-4 AP action (4 AP at -0, 3 AP at -25, or 2 AP at -50). If grappled by multiple attackers, rolls must be made to escape from each one, although the total penalty applies to all rolls, and no additional AP are required (a single action permits a roll against each attacker). The grap ple is automatically broken if the attacker is stunned, incapacitated, or decides to release the defender.

As with other conflicting maneuvers, the amount the attacker rolls over 100 is subtracted from the target’s roll to escape and the result resolved as a Percentage Maneuver. The grapple is reduced by the Percentage Maneuver result.

Example: Aldrik has chased a cultist into a cave, where he is attacked by an entangling vine and the cultist himself. The vine achieves a 10% grapple and the cultist 20%. Aldrik spends the full round (4 AP) attempting to break free. Aldrik is a passable wrestler with a +43 Wrestling skill. Since the vine is a plant, the gamemaster considers it a Medium maneuver to escape (+0). The vine has a grappling OB of +30 and rolls 35 for a total of 65, adding 100 – 65 = 35 to Aldrik’s roll of 63 and his skill for a total of 141, minus the total grapple amount of 30. The final result of 111 gives a result of 100 on the percentage maneuver table, so he is free of the plant.

At the same time, Aldrik is trying to break free from the cultist. Since they weigh approximately the same amount, each is trying to control 100% of their own weight, resulting in a Very Hard (-20) maneuver for both combatants. The cultist rolls an 88 with a skill of 60, minus 20, subtracting 28 from Aldrik’s new roll. Aldrik’s roll to break free is a roll of 47 +

43 skill -20 for the Very Hard maneuver -28 for his foe’s roll -30 for the grapple amounts, for a total of 12. This yields a result of 10 on the percentage ma neuver table, reducing the cultist’s grip from a 20% grapple to just 10%, but Aldrik is not free yet.

Until the grapple is broken, the attacker and defender are bound together, and neither can move away with out a contested Feat of Strength (Section 5.2) based on Weight-Training skill to move their foe. This Feat is part of their movement action and does not require any additional expenditure of AP, but also will not break the grapple; if successful, the foe is dragged along, unless the attacker chooses to let go.

Example: Anika, a 210 lbs warrior from the snowy north, is grappled by a 93 lbs hobgoblin, who has somehow managed to achieve a 40% grapple. She has better things to do and tries to walk away. The hobgoblin is 44% of her weight, making it a Medi um maneuver for her, while she is 225% of the hob goblin, putting him at an Absurd (-70) maneuver.

The hobgoblin rolls 67 plus his skill of 50, minus 70 for a total of 47, adding 53 to Anika’s roll. Wrestling isn’t her strongest suit, but she has a +47 skill and rolls a 61, plus +0 for a Medium maneuver and +53 from the hobgoblin’s roll, minus 40 for the grapple, for a total of 121, which yields a 100 result on the percentage maneuver table. The hobgoblin isn’t even slowing her down.

Hits

Hits are most often inflicted from the Attack Table, however critical effects can also cause hits. A com batant is knocked unconscious when he drops to 0 or fewer hits, but he takes penalties to all actions once more than 25% of total hits are lost, as shown in Table 9-8b. Death occurs when a character has taken hits beyond zero that exceed that character’s Body Devel opment skill bonus. (Thus, a character with a 50 bonus in Body Development skill dies at -51 hits.)

Table 9-8b: Hit Loss Penalties
Hits Lost Penalty
0 - 25% 0
26 - 50% -10
51 - 75% -20
76% + -30

ROLEMASTER

UNIFIED



Arms Law

Penalties

Many injuries result in a penalty, which is applied to absolute and percentage maneuvers, spellcast ing, attacks, and full or partial blocking or dodging.

Penalties represent the loss of functionality (whereas pain is represented by hit loss and its corresponding penalties). Penalties from all injuries stack, although if the affected area has nothing to do at all with the ma neuver then the penalty should not apply at all. If the affected area is not being directly used, but does have some role, then half the penalty may be appropriate.

When in doubt apply the full sum of all penalties. All injury penalties last until the injury is healed, although the penalty diminishes over time (Section 13.1).

Example: Flinky’s -10 for a broken finger does not apply to jumping over the pit, and her -20 from broken toes does not apply to picking a lock on the door further down the hall. Trying to pull the heavy, ROLEMASTER somewhat stuck door open would face the full -30

UNIFIED penalty for both.



Arms Law

The sum of a character’s penalties generally reflects how broken he is. Penalties of -50 or more mean a character is having serious problems, and should be role-played that way. As penalties approach -100 char acters are approaching incapacitation.

Once penalties reach -100, the character is in serious distress. Any action that requires effort (movement, at tacks, spell casting or any maneuvers requiring phys ical effort) will provoke a fatigue check for the round.

This will continue until penalties have been reduced to a lower severity than -100. You need to sit down and rest, and any action will tend to make things worse.

At total penalties of -200 or more, the character is dying from his collective injuries, and must make a fatigue check every round, even if lying still. This will continue until total penalties are reduced below -200.

Once penalties reach or exceed -300, the character will die on the next upkeep phase due to systemic shock.

Staggered

Staggered means that the combatant has been knocked off balance. A staggered result immediately loses 1 Ac tion Point, either this round if the target has any left, or the beginning of the following round. A staggered combatant may still parry, as long as the last Action Point he spent was on a melee action, but his OB (and consequently his Parry) may be reduced if fewer Action Points are available to be spent on his attack action. Any in-progress action may be continued upon the combat ant’s next opportunity to expend an Action Point.

Example: Cael is firing his bow, starting in the first Action Phase. After spending his second AP in the second Action Phase, he is struck by a thrown rock and Staggered. He loses his AP in the third Action Phase, but is able to complete firing his bow in the fourth Action Phase, having expended a total of three AP.

Example: Lexxa begins her round with a Perception check (2 AP) and spots a foe hiding in the bush es. On the third Action Phase she begins a melee attack, but is struck from behind and Staggered. She loses her fourth AP and is unable to complete the attack. If she had only spent one AP on Perception and started her attack in the second Action Phase, she would have been able to resolve her attack at the very end of the round, at a penalty of -50 for a 2 AP melee attack.

Prone

cessful Acrobatics maneuver).

Knocked Back

Being knocked back will move the character a distance in feet in a given direction, as indicated by the critical result. Depending on the terrain the combatant may suffer additional effects. Being knocked over an edge or into a stairwell/steep slope may provoke a fall, being knocked into water or fire may provoke skill checks damage on the person and/or a breakage check on the object crashed into (depending on their relative sturdi ness). The GM should use his discretion, but even the wall or a rack of spears should not inflict a critical of a higher severity level than the one that provoked the knockback (use an A if in doubt).

Unless the target is knocked unconscious and knocked back, the GM should allow the knocked back target a maneuver roll (using either the appropriate movement ficulty determined by the GM based on the situation. character, assume the target sees him coming; if he comes from the flank or behind, have the target make a Perception maneuver to see the knocked back charac ter coming at him (difficulty based on how chaotic the situation is). The GM should determine the difficulty of getting out of the way based on the specifics of the situation and make a maneuver using Acrobatics or the appropriate Movement skill to get out of the way.

Location is fuzzy enough within “combat radius” that this maneuver should be considered an instantaneous action. If they fail to move out of the way, they are knocked prone.

Stun

Prone means the target has been thrown flat to the floor A stun means the combatant has been knocked hard and is subject to the usual modifiers to action for prone enough to disorient and put him in a vulnerable state. position, and he must take a 2 Action Point action to The effect is a temporary penalty each round, either stand up again (this may be reduced to 1 AP with a suc -25, -50, or -75, as indicated in the critical, representing different amounts of disorientation: • [-25]: A minor disorientation where the combatant is dizzy and unstable.

• [-50]: The combatant has been hit seriously enough to disrupt his senses, and he is struggling to control his body.

• [-75]: Suffering from a serious blow, the combatant is having a hard time remaining standing and nearly at his foe’s mercy. or criticals. Being knocked into an object may inflict All stun results are cumulative, with the total rounds of Stun[-25] adding together, Stun[-50] adding together, and so on. One round of stun effect wears off at the end of the round, from the worst effect to the least (-75 worst case scenario of being thrown directly into a solid rounds wear off first, then -50, then -25).

Example: A few rounds into a battle, Taor has 2 rounds of Stun[-50] and 1 round of Stun[-25]. Un fortunately he then gets hit from behind, resulting in one round of Stun[-75] and 1 round of Stun[-25].

He now will be at -75 for 1 round, then -50 for two rounds, then -25 for 2 rounds. Poor Taor! skill or Acrobatics) to attempt to catch himself at a dif Stunned characters are in a state of mental confusion due to a blow, and players should be encouraged to role- If the “object” the combatant is knocked back into is play this state. Any maneuvers related to memories of another aware entity, both the combatant and the tar events that happened during a stunned round should be get may end up harmed in the collision. If the knocked made at -50. This is true even years later; the character back combatant is coming from the front of the second simply lacks a clear memory of those events.

Example: During the fight, Elsarde was stunned when the priest shouted a threat at the group of PCs attacking the temple. After the fight, Elsarde asks the GM what the priest was shouting. The GM has her make a Perception maneuver to see if she was paying attention to such details, which is at -50 because she was stunned. Alas, she fails: “I dun no Victor, that whole middle of the fight is kind of hazy.” Some creatures may be immune to stun (as noted in the creature description), however they are generally not immune to the knocking around that comes with a good stun. Instead, the target suffers a flat -25 for the remain der of the round (regardless of the severity of stun), or the following round if he has no Action Points left to ROLEMASTER spend. The penalty does not stack with additional stuns, UNIFIED  it can only be a -25 for 1 round.

Arms Law

Example: Borin attacks a Skeleton as it comes charging at him and rolls a critical that deals Stun[- 75] for 5 rounds. The Skeleton, being immune to stuns, is knocked off-balance and is at -25 for the rest of the round and will suffer no penalty thereafter.

9.9. Extended Example

Kirax and Thyrzia have just started work as bounty hunters (see their stats in the examples of Chapter 2). They are on the tail of Harshak, a notorious goat rustler. It's the first bounty they're trying to pick up as a team, so it's not exactly the most glamor ous, but hopefully it will lead to something bigger.

They've tracked Harshak to a camp in the woods, where they see Harshak and his partners Gavart and Caitsley are getting ready to raid a nearby homestead for livestock.

Kirax uses his Survival skill to make an educated guess which route they will take, rolling a 77 plus his skill of 39 for a total of 116, success. Then he and Thyrzia use Stalking to lay an ambush, with one of them on each side of the trail. The goat rustlers fail to notice them during their initial approach, but the gamemaster gives them another chance during the ambush. Stalking is resolved as a conflicting skill. Kirax rolls 45 plus his skill of 43, for a total of 88. This results in a bonus of 12 to the Perception rolls of those attempting to notice him. Thyrzia does better, rolling 80 plus her skill of 29 for 109, which will subtract 9 from those Perception rolls. Gavart, in the lead, rolls 28 plus his skill of 32, and notices neither bounty hunter. Harshak, in the rear, rolls 36 plus his skill of 40; he gets a partial success to notice Kirax but completely fails to notice Thyrzia.

Caitsley, in the middle, rolls 77 with her skill of 30; she notices Kirax and thinks there might be some one else in the area.

At this point the gamemaster moves to combat rounds as conflict is imminent. The first round starts with Gavart in striking range of Thyrzia and Cait sley in range of Kirax, with Harshak further back on the trail. Initiative is Thyrzia first with 23, then Caitsley (13), Harshak (10), Kirax (9), and Gavart (3). Thyrzia and Kirax are within 10' of their targets but do not know the outcome of their quarry’s perception rolls. On the first phase, they each begin attacks, with Thyrzia moving to Gavart and Kirax moving to Caitsley. Moving at a Creep pace (with no pace penalty), they will reach their foes by the end of the round. At this point, they notice none of their foes have reacted to them, so they have at least achieved surprise, although they do not yet know to ROLEMASTER

UNIFIED what degree.



Arms Law

On the second phase, Kirax and Thyrzia each have the option to resolve a quick attack (2 AP) at -50, hitting before their foes have any chance to react, or they can continue a full attack and hope they have not been spotted. They decide to have faith in their plan and continue the attack. On the third phase, Thyrzia continues her attack against Gavart. Caits ley, who was surprised but not flat-footed, can now act. She draws a broadsword and makes a run for it, moving 15' (a Run pace). She does not take a defen sive action (e.g. making an attack so she can parry, or a partial dodge), because the -75 pace penalty would render those actions moot based on her skill bonuses (since her OB is +55 and her Running skill, for dodging, is +40).

Kirax has another choice. Caitsley was in his melee range, due to the reach of his two-handed spear, so he can either continue his full 4 AP attack pursuing at a run (with a -75 pace penalty), or he can take his attack now (at -25 for a 3 AP attack). Either way, it will be a rear attack (+35), but he's lost his chance for a Surprise bonus or to use his Ambush skill; for those, he would have needed to attack in the 2nd phase before she reacted. Kirax decides to attack now. The attack occurs on Caitsley's ini tiative, but uses Kirax's action point for the third phase. The total modifiers are +60 (his skill) +10 (for a two-handed weapon) +35 (for a rear attack)

-25 (for a 3 AP attack), totaling +80. He could reserve part of this bonus for parrying, but with surprised foes, he decides that's unnecessary. His attack roll is 59 for a total of 139. Caitsley is wear ing AT3, and this is a Big attack (because the spear is two-handed), so the result is 23 D Puncture.

He rolls a 44 for the critical, a thrust to the knee damaging the joint and causing serious bleeding.

The effect is bleeding 4 hits per round, a -5 injury penalty, a -10 fatigue penalty, and two rounds of Stun[ -25]. Caitsley stumbles away in serious pain.

Harshak, with a partial success on his perception roll, has an idea something is going on. He spends the third phase making a 1 AP perception roll to fig ure out exactly what, and easily notices Kirax now that the attack has begun.

Kirax would be next in the initiative, but he already used his action for the phase against Caitsley. Ga vart is last, and still flat-footed and oblivious. He'll be able to act next round.

On the fourth phase, Thyrzia resolves her attack against Gavart. She's armed with a light mace and a hand axe. She opts to attack with both. The base penalty for two attacks is -75, but since both weap ons are light, it is reduced to -55, and her Multiple Attacks skill of 20 reduces it to -35. (She’s added this up in advance, since it is a tactic she plans to use whenever she has the advantage.) She also has the ambidextrous talent, eliminating any off-hand penalty. With Gavart flat-footed, he has no defen sive bonus, and she gains a bonus of +35 for a rear attack and +25 for surprise. Her total bonus based on her OB of +39 is +64. She considers making a subduing attack, but that's one too many penalties and she decides against. She rolls a 41 for the first attack for 105, and an 86 on the second for 150.

Gavart is in AT3, and her attacks are Small because they are light weapons, so the mace scores a 7AK and the hand axe 16DS. For the Krush critical, she rolls a 55 and adds her Ambush ranks for a total of 59, cracking a bone in his hand for 14 hits, bleeding 1 hit per round, and a -5 injury penalty.

The Slashing critical is a 58, modified to 62, hitting Gavart in the elbow for 32 more hits, a -15 penalty, and a round of Stun[-25]. Gavart has taken a total of 69 damage. That's more than he has, so he falls unconscious. If the bleeding is not stopped, he will eventually die.

Caitsley has the next initiative and keeps running.

The gamemaster rules the forest is Hard (-10) ter rain to move in. Caitsley is pretty spry, and normal ly a roll might not be needed, but now she has a -5 injury penalty to one leg, a -10 fatigue penalty, and is Stunned[-25]. He rolls to see how she does, roll ing 02 for an open-ended down result, followed by an 8. Her Running skill is +40, so the final total is -6, resolved as a percentage maneuver. She stumbles and fails to get anywhere, but manages not to hurt herself any further.

Harshak unslings a spear and starts backing up.

Kirax advances on Harshak. With only one action phase left, he can't start an attack, but he can at least close the distance. Gavart gurgles a bit.

At the end of the round, Gavart loses a hit due to bleeding, and Caitsley loses four. Caitsley is still stunned, since she had spent 3 action points before being stunned last round.

In the second round, Thyrzia is first again (16), then Harshak (12) and Kirax are tied (12), and Caitsley is last (1) in part due to -3 from her various penal ties.

Thyrzia moves towards Harshak. Rather than closing, she decides to throw her hand axe. Harshak and Kirax both begin attacks against each other.

Caitsley swears and tries to get a bandage around her bleeding wound (using direct pressure). Thyr zia's action will be resolved in the third phase, and everyone else in the fourth phase, so the gamemas ter skips forward. Thyrzia throws her axe; 32 plus her skill of 38 for a 70. It whizzes by Harshak's head, barely missing him.

In the fourth phase, Thyrzia draws another hand axe, and Kirax and Harshak make simultaneous attacks against each other. Kirax applies 15 OB to his parry, while Harshak makes a reckless all-out attack. Kirax rolls a 95 for 153, while Harshak rolls a 66 for 111. Kirax deals a 26EP and Harshak a 17CP. Kirax's critical is a 97, tearing through Harshak's chest and guaranteeing a slow, painful death (and immediate incapacitation). Harshak rolls a 62, stabbing Kirax in the shoulder for 14 hits, a -10 injury penalty, and bleeding 4 hits per round.

Also in the fourth phase, Caitsley succeeds at a Medicine maneuver to stop her bleeding, but it will take four minutes of treatment to keep from immedi ately opening up on her. She calls out, "I... surren der?" Kirax, with his own injury, is happy to accept that and let her go. The bounty was on Harshak. It would have paid better if he was alive, but, well, not much to be done about that now.

UNIFIED



Arms Law



Arms Law

Attack TablesAttack Tables

10. Attack Tables10. Attack Tables

Tables for each type of attack are given on their own Small and Big Tables

page, including typical modifiers and statistics for the The optional Small and Big tables incorporate both attack as well as optional Small and Big versions of a critical severity adjustment (one level, appropriate the attack table. for Small or Big attacks against Medium targets) and For each attack type, the following information is list an adjustment to the number of hits delivered (x0.75 ed: for Small and x1.5 for Big). If the adjustment to hits • Criticals: The types of criticals that may be inflicted delivered is not desired, use only the Medium tables. by the attack, including the letter used to denote If using the optional hit adjustments for larger and the critical type on the attack tables. smaller attacks, for attacks smaller than Small or larg

• Disarm modifier: The penalty to Disarm attempts, er than Big use the Medium table and multiply the hits from Table 9-7a in Section 9.7. delivered by the factor on Table 9-4a.

• Subdual modifier: The penalty to Subdual attempts, If the target of the attack is not Medium, modify the from Table 9-7e in Section 9.7. critical severity according to the target size as shown

• Size: For weapons and natural attacks, the size is on Table 9-4a. For example, if an attack is made given as a modifier. A +0 size is an attack of the against a Big target, reduce the critical severity by one same size as the attacker (typically Medium). A level. The Small and Big tables already incorporate +1 size is an attack one size larger, e.g., a Medi the adjustment for the attack size. um attacker with a +1 size attack will make a Big Due to the critical severity adjustment for size, the attack. For spells, the size is given as the actual Small tables include criticals of less than A severity. attack size (e.g., Medium), as the size of the spell These are denoted as Z. Roll an A critical, but find the is independent of the size of the caster. next lower result that affects the same area of the body

• Length: The length of a typical weapon. This also on the A column and apply it. defines the wielder’s melee range (half the char acter’s height plus the length of the weapon). For weapons other than Medium size, multiply the length by 1.6 for each size larger, or divide by 1.6 for each size smaller.

• Strength: The strength of a typical weapon as ap plied to breakage checks (Section 6.1).

• Weight: The weight of a typical weapon. For weap ons other than Medium size, multiply the weight by 4 for each size larger, or divide by 4 for each size smaller.

• Fumble: Any unmodified roll between 1 and the Fumble number results in a fumble. The fumble number is reduced by 1 for every 5 ranks the character has in their applicable attack skill, to a minimum of 1.

• Range: For ranged attacks, the maximum range for each range increment. No modifier applies for attacks up to the listed Short range, a -10 modi fier applies for attacks beyond Short range up to Medium range, etc. Crossbows also have a Point Blank range within which they gain a +20 bonus.

Optionally, range may be adjusted based on the attack size (see Section 9.4).

ROLEMASTER

Chapter 11:Chapter 11:

UNIFIED



Arms Law

Critical TablesCritical Tables

Most critical effects are summarized with symbols below the description. Any number before the symbol indicates the duration (in rounds unless otherwise noted), while any number after the symbol indicates severity (e.g., hits, penalty, distance). For details, see Section 9.8.

O +X: Additional X hits of damage X: Bleeding, at X hits every round (-X): Fatigue penalty of X  or (-X): Breakage roll at –X (if given)

-X or T(-X): Injury penalty of X for T time period T  [-25/-50/-75]: Stunned at the given penalty for T rounds or timespan.

 or  (X): Staggered (loses 1 AP or X, if given)

 X': Knocked back distance of X' : Knocked prone/pushed to ground  X%: Grappled X% 💀: Target is dead, dying, or otherwise defeated (unable or unwilling to fight)

(X): Additional criticals of X severity

Critical Tables

Some critical tables represent common types of weap on strikes, some represent special types of attack, and others describe the effects of different types of energy or magic. The following critical tables are provided:

This may be from enchanted creatures that secrete or expel acid as a defense mechanism.

• Cold: Elemental cold attacks such as from spells or magical effects inflict cold criticals, and extreme cold conditions may do so as well. (The abbrevia avoid confusion with C severity criticals.)

• Electricity: Most often from enchanted attacks or creatures, lightning criticals could also be inflicted from electrical devices or natural lightning. (The confusion with E severity criticals.)

ROLEMASTER

UNIFIED • Grapple: Grapple criticals are usually inflicted by  unarmed attacks that focus on grabbing and hold Arms Law ing. They may also come from some weapons such as whips.

• Heat: Natural extreme fire and heat, as well as en chanted elemental heat attacks such as fire balls,

224 inflict heat criticals.

11. Critical Tables11. Critical Tables

• Holy: Weapons or spells from a divine source, such as from a godly being or from someone channeling the power of a godly being, may cause holy crit icals. Holy weapons usually inflict holy criticals in addition to the normal type from the weapon itself.

• Impact: Blunt force that covers large areas of the body, such as a falling block, a fall, or ram attack, cause impact criticals.

• Krush: Blunt crushing weapons like maces and clubs cause krush criticals. (Krush criticals are abbreviated as K on the attack tables to avoid con fusion with C severity criticals.)

• Puncture: Piercing attacks come from pointed weapons like arrows, stabbing blades like dag gers, or from natural weaponry like stingers.

• Slash: Slashing criticals involve a sharp edge cut ting the target, and are most often used by blades such as swords and axes.

• Steam: Heated water vapor can be scalding and in flict serious burns, from either natural or enchant ed sources.

• Strikes: Unarmed attacks that focus on punching and kicking are like crushing attacks causing bruising, stuns, and concussion damage, but rarely breaking the skin except with the most serious of blows. (Unarmed Strikes criticals are abbreviated as T on the attack tables.)

• Acid: Caustic or acidic attacks inflict acid criticals. • Sweeps: Unarmed attacks primarily using throws and sweeping attacks are meant to redirect and use the foe’s weight against him. These attacks unbalance the foe and can also cause serious blunt force damage through dropping the foe or impacts with objects or walls. tion for Cold criticals on the attack tables is O, to • Subdual: While subdual attacks are always meant to be non-lethal, death is still a possibility, as is serious permanent injury. Most injuries however tend to be temporary, such as fatigue, stunning, or short duration penalties. abbreviation for Electricity criticals is L, to avoid • Unbalance: Severe hits from melee attacks often will cause secondary criticals in addition to the normal critical type. The force behind these blows can seriously unbalance the foe causing him to fall or otherwise injure himself.

 GameMaster LawGameMaster Law

Gamemaster Law

Gamemaster LawGamemaster Law

Character Law provides the roles to be In Chapter 14, the psychology and inter played as well as the skills and the ma actions of non-player characters are de neuvers to use them in. Arms Law offers scribed, providing guidance in determining the characters the challenge of battle, with their reactions. Players may need to per its risks and dangers. Here, Gamemaster suade, inspire, or deceive them to achieve Law provides the remaining tools a game their goals, depending on whether they are master will need to create their stories and allies, adversaries, or simply inhabitants of adventures. the world the Gamemaster is creating.

Chapter 12 provides the tools for design The final chapter describes hazards of the ing a campaign, defining the tone and environment. These include extremes of power level of the game, and customizing temperature, drowning, falling, poison, the races, cultures, and professions to suit disease, and darkness. the setting. Guidelines are provided for ex Being the Gamemaster of a roleplaying perience points, to reward players for ac game involves a lot of separate jobs rolled complishing tasks and advancing the story. up into one. The GM must direct the action, Chapter 13 covers health, healing, and act out all the non-player roles, resolve death, including the treatment of injuries disputes, and keep the flow of the game and their natural recovery. moving. Stepping back from the action, the GM also writes all the elements of the story not controlled by the players. Role master can be used as the basis for many different stories, and the rules in these sec tions will help the GM get the job done at the gaming table and customize the game to fit the style of action he wants to run.
